title: Quick check: clearing an offline backlog

questions.prompt: 'The number on the dashboard is a count, not a problem.' What does this mean in practice?
choices: Twelve offlines split as nine powered-off.

choices.label: The count is fictional and should be ignored.
choices.rationale: The count is real; it just isn't the whole picture.

choices.label: Always aim to drive the count to zero by any means.
choices.rationale: The 'make the number drop' anti-pattern.

choices.label: The count is the customer's problem, not the MSP's.
choices.rationale: The MSP owns categorisation and remediation.

questions.explanation: Two network-blocked. One uninstalled is a 25% real-work load. Twelve offlines that are all uninstalled is a five-alarm fire. The count doesn't tell you which. The count is a signal that needs categorisation before it becomes meaningful.

questions.prompt: What's the rule for batching backlog fixes?
choices: Batching is allowed within a category.

choices.label: Never batch fixes; always per-endpoint.
choices.rationale: Within-category batching is explicit.

choices.label: Always batch across causes to save time.
choices.rationale: The named anti-pattern.

choices.label: Batch only on Mondays.
choices.rationale: Not the framing.

questions.explanation: Not across. Re-pushing to ten uninstalled agents in one RMM job is fine; re-pushing to ten offline agents (mixed causes) is the make-the-number-drop failure mode. The categorisation discipline is what makes batching safe.

questions.prompt: 'Re-pushing to every offline agent indiscriminately to make the number drop' — what's the critique?
choices: Wrong fix for three of the four causes.

choices.label: Re-pushing is fine; the lesson exaggerates the risk.
choices.rationale: The risk (RMM churn, false 'fixed' state) is the lesson's point.

choices.label: Re-pushing voids the customer's contract.
choices.rationale: Not a real commercial mechanic.

choices.label: Re-pushing is what the runbook calls for.
choices.rationale: Wrong on the runbook framing.

questions.explanation: Doesn't change anything for two of them (powered off, network blocked). Creates churn in the RMM. The category-then-fix discipline takes longer up-front and finishes faster overall. Indiscriminate bulk re-push is the canonical make-the-number-drop failure.

questions.prompt: What's the leverage point when working a categorised backlog?
choices: Look for patterns within the categorisation.

choices.label: Fix the largest category first regardless of cause.
choices.rationale: Volume isn't the leverage; pattern is.

choices.label: Always fix in alphabetical order of hostname.
choices.rationale: Arbitrary; not a real workflow.

choices.label: Always escalate the entire backlog to a senior.
choices.rationale: Bypasses the tech's diagnostic responsibility.

questions.explanation: Five service-stopped on the same OU, three uninstalled on the same site. Shared cause, shared fix. One investigation, one fix, many endpoints unstick.

questions.prompt: Monday morning: 14 offline agents on a customer. The account manager wants the count down before Thursday's review. What's your first move?

choices.label: Re-push the install to all 14 endpoints.
choices.rationale: Reaching for bulk re-push without categorising is the make-the-number-drop failure mode.

choices: Sort the offline list, run the two-question diagnostic on each.

choices.label: Ask the account manager to push the weekly review back.
choices.rationale: The work is doable inside the window if you categorise first.

questions.explanation: Capture the categorisation in a worksheet. Then plan the fix sequence by category. Two minutes per endpoint of categorisation prevents the half-day of misapplied fixes.

questions.prompt: Categorisation: eight powered-off (weekend/leave/decommissioned), three service-stopped (all same OU, Windows update yesterday), two uninstalled (executive laptops, SCCM cleanup task in logs), one network-blocked (remote worker on hotel WiFi captive portal). What's the right action set?
choices: Start the service on the three;

choices.label: Apply the blocked-outbound firewall fix to all 14.
choices.rationale: Only one of the 14 is network-blocked, and that one's network isn't your customer's network.

choices.label: Re-push the install to the two uninstalled and ignore the rest.
choices.rationale: Half-right; ignoring service-stopped means they recur with the same Windows-update cause.

questions.explanation: Document SCCM exclusion fix for the executive laptops then re-push; tell the remote worker the captive portal blocks outbound and coverage resumes on a clean network; document the eight powered-off as no-action. Each category gets the right fix; shared causes are named in the documentation.

questions.prompt: Why is 'stopping documentation to save time' called out as a mistake?
choices: Same backlog appears next month with no record of what fixed which last time.

choices.label: Documentation is required by Huntress for billing.
choices.rationale: Not a real commercial mechanic.

choices.label: Undocumented fixes trigger SOC alerts.
choices.rationale: Not how the SOC operates.

choices.label: Documentation is purely cosmetic.
choices.rationale: Documentation is what makes the work durable.

questions.explanation: Documentation is what makes the backlog smaller over time. Skipping documentation makes the next pass identical to this one.

questions.prompt: What's the right shape of the customer-facing one-line summary?
choices: 'X endpoints powered off; Y service-stopped and restored;

choices.label: 'Count dropped from 14 to 0.'
choices.rationale: The make-the-number-drop framing the lesson pushes back on.

choices.label: 'All 14 resolved.' No detail.
choices.rationale: The customer can't act on this; the categories drive whether next month's backlog is preventable.

choices.label: Send the full categorisation worksheet without summary.
choices.rationale: Too much detail for the executive summary.

questions.explanation: Z network-blocked and now resolved at the firewall; W uninstalled and re-pushed.' Not just a number, the categories matter. Categories give the customer the actual picture, not just a count.

questions.prompt: The backlog includes endpoints in the 'In AD, not in RMM' category, endpoints the MSP shouldn't be covering at all that somehow ended up in scope. What's the right move?

choices.label: Uninstall the agents quietly and don't mention it.
choices.rationale: Hides the underlying scope question that the senior needs to settle with the customer.

choices.label: Add them to the RMM and treat them as standard backlog.
choices.rationale: Scope must be settled before they enter the backlog properly.

choices.label: Open a Huntress support ticket asking what to do.
choices.rationale: Not a Huntress question; it's an MSP-internal deployment-strategy question.

questions.explanation: Scope decisions are explicitly escalation surface.

questions.prompt: Order the categories in the 'apply fixes by category' sequence.
items: Powered off: no action; note in PSA, move on.
Service stopped: start the service via RMM; if same agents drop again within an hour, investigate why; don't loop on restarts.
Network blocked: lesson 17 diagnostic; one network-level fix can clear multiple endpoints at once.
Uninstalled: investigate why before re-pushing; if a shared cause, fix the cause first, then re-push the batch.
questions.explanation: Cheapest to most expensive: no-op, restart, network fix, cause-investigate-then-re-push.
